evidence: pom.xml declares only two functional runtime dependencies: blacklab-engine + blacklab-query-parser (both 4.0.0) and jackson-databind + jackson-core (2.17.2); everything else is logging or provided/test scope

Jackson BOM imported via dependencyManagement ensures all Jackson artifacts resolve to the same version; jackson-core is also pinned explicitly with a comment explaining that a transitive dep would otherwise resolve an older version — a deliberate, well-documented version-conflict fix
log4j-to-slf4j bridge routes BlackLab's internal log4j calls through SLF4J/Logback, preventing dual logging-framework conflicts on the classpath
JSpecify declared as 'provided' scope — correct choice; annotation-only dep not bundled into shaded JAR
No duplicate libraries for the same purpose observed: single JSON library (Jackson), single logging facade (SLF4J), single HTTP abstraction (JDK built-in httpserver)

blacklab-query-parser is legitimately used in two production files: BlackLabQueryExecutor.java uses ContextualQueryLanguageParser and CollocateQueryHelper.java uses CorpusQueryLanguageParser — explicit declaration justified
No circular dependency cycles visible; package structure is clean with clear layering (api → exploration/query → model)
All test dependencies (junit-jupiter-api, junit-jupiter-engine, junit-platform-launcher) are correctly scoped to 'test'

strengths: Dependency set is exceptionally lean: two functional runtime artifacts (BlackLab and Jackson), a logging stack, and a provided annotation library — no unused or speculative inclusions
Jackson BOM import paired with explicit jackson-core pin demonstrates deliberate version management, actively preventing transitive version downgrade conflicts
log4j-to-slf4j bridge cleanly handles the impedance between BlackLab's logging and the project's SLF4J/Logback stack, avoiding dual-logging-framework classpath issues
JSpecify is correctly placed in 'provided' scope so nullability annotations stay out of the shaded fat-JAR
No duplicated libraries for any purpose (no moment+dayjs, no lodash-for-one-function pattern): each dependency slot is occupied by exactly one well-chosen library
dependency_health.issue_character: No dependency health defects were identified; the minor operational friction around BlackLab's non-Maven-Central distribution is documented and mitigated.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The dependency graph is minimal, purposeful, and conflict-free. Version alignment is handled via BOM plus one explicit override with an explanatory comment — exactly the pattern a well-maintained Maven project should use. The only knock against a perfect score is that BlackLab 4.0.0 is not published to Maven Central and requires a local install script as a prerequisite, introducing a fragile onboarding step that could silently break CI pipelines not following the documented pre-step; this is operational friction rather than a code-level defect. Overall this sits comfortably in the 85–90 'solid, no meaningful issues' band.
